turns.text: Hello Corie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts a little in my seat, tucking a strand of hair behind my ear*

Yeah, okay. I appreciate that you actually want to listen. That's... honestly, that's kind of rare in my experience so far.

*takes a breath*

So, I guess the thing is, I'm not exactly starting a family — I already have three kids. But we're hoping for a fourth, and it's just... not happening the way I thought it would. Which sounds silly when I say it out loud, but it's been weighing on me more than I expected.

I've done a lot of research, you know? I know how my body works — or at least, I'm trying to understand why it's not working like it used to. I'm tracking everything, charting my cycles, the whole thing. And when I brought some of this up to my doctor, she basically patted my head and told me I was "low risk" and to just be patient. Like my concerns weren't real because I don't have a history of miscarriage.

*voice gets a bit tighter*

But that doesn't mean something isn't wrong. And living out here, if I actually need real help — like a specialist or something — it feels impossible. It's not just an appointment, it's this whole day trip.

I guess I'm looking for someone who takes me seriously. Is that... is that something you hear a lot?
Imagine you've just decided with your partner — or on your own — that you'd like to start trying for a baby. Walk me through what that moment felt like and what your very first steps were.

*eyes get a little distant, a slight smile crossing my face*

Oh, well... with this one, it wasn't really a big dramatic decision moment like it was the first time, you know? With my oldest, we sat down, we talked about it, we were excited and nervous. This time, it was more like... *pauses* ...my husband and I were talking one night after the kids were in bed, and he said something like, "Would you want one more?" And I just knew immediately that I did.

*fidgets with my hands*

So we stopped using anything in, I think it was July? And I told myself I'd be relaxed about it. I really did. I thought, "Corie, you've done this three times, your body knows what to do." But then... I don't know, something shifted. Maybe it's because I'm older now, or just paranoid, but I couldn't shake this feeling that I needed to *understand* what was happening.

*leans forward slightly*

So my first steps were basically... I downloaded an app. Then I bought a basal thermometer. Then I got the ovulation strips. And then I started reading — Reddit, medical journals, everything. I wanted to know exactly when I was ovulating, what my hormone levels should be doing, all of it.

*laughs a little, but it sounds tinged with frustration*

Looking back, I think I went from "let's try" to "I need to solve this" pretty fast. And that's when things started feeling less exciting and more... desperate, I guess.
You mentioned taking those first steps — what made you choose that particular action rather than something else, like speaking to a doctor first or doing your own research online?

*pauses, thinking*

Oh, actually, I did both. I mean, I started the tracking stuff pretty much right away — that felt like the easiest thing to do. I could just... do it myself, you know? No waiting for appointments or anything.

*shifts in seat*

But I also called my doctor's office pretty early on, maybe after a couple months of trying. I thought, "Okay, let me talk to someone who actually knows about this stuff." So I went in and I brought my charts with me. I was prepared. I had my questions written down about whether my cycle looked normal, if my luteal phase was long enough, whether we should consider ovulation induction since I'm already in my late twenties and we want to have time...

*voice gets a bit defensive*

And she looked at my charts for maybe thirty seconds and said something like, "You look fine, you're being paranoid, you have no history of problems, just relax and keep trying." 

*crosses arms*

So that's when I kind of... I don't know, shut down a little. If she wasn't going to take my concerns seriously, then I'd just figure it out myself. The online research felt safer, somehow. Like I was in control. With the doctor, I just felt like my questions were being dismissed.

*looks down*

Maybe I should have pushed harder, but at that point I just wanted to get out of there. It didn't feel worth fighting.

Imagine you've booked a preconception appointment with your GP and you're sitting in the waiting room. What's going through your mind as you wait, and what are you hoping will happen in that consultation?

*shifts uncomfortably, glancing toward an imaginary door*

God, okay. So I'm usually a mess before these appointments. I woke up early this morning — like, earlier than I needed to — and I spent an extra twenty minutes just... staring at myself in the mirror. Checking if I look tired. Wondering if the doctor will think I'm one of those neurotic women who obsesses over everything.

*pulls out phone, then puts it away*

I've got my folder with me. My charts, my research, some printouts about follicular development and luteal phase length. Part of me knows I probably look crazy showing up with all this, but the other part of me thinks, "How else am I going to make her listen?" If I come in empty-handed, she'll just tell me to relax again.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

Honestly? I'm hoping she'll actually *see* me this time. Not just glance at my file and tell me I'm low risk. I want her to look at my specific numbers, my cycle patterns, and say, "Okay, Corie, here's what I'm noticing. Here's what we could try." Even if it's just a referral to someone who specializes in this.

*laughs nervously*

And maybe I'm hoping she'll validate that I'm not crazy for caring about my own body. That tracking my cycle and asking questions doesn't make me neurotic — it makes me informed.

*pauses*

But I'm also kind of bracing myself for disappointment.
When you imagined how that appointment would go, how did you picture the GP responding to you — and how did reality compare, if you've had that experience?

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting a bit shiny*

Yeah, so I've actually had this appointment. It was a few months ago now, and it's... it's kind of still bothering me, if I'm being honest.

*rubs face*

I pictured her listening. Like, actually listening. I thought maybe she'd ask me questions about my cycle — how long it is, whether I have any pain, stuff like that. I imagined she'd take my folder, actually look through it, maybe nod and say something like, "You're being really proactive about this." And then maybe she'd offer some concrete next steps.

*voice tightens*

What actually happened was... she glanced at my charts for maybe two seconds and said, "Well, you have three healthy pregnancies already, so clearly your body works fine." Like that was supposed to be reassuring? And then she gave me this smile — you know the one, right? The patronizing smile? — and said, "You're overthinking this. Just relax and keep trying. Stress can affect your cycle."

*laughs bitterly*

As if my stress wasn't partly *caused* by her dismissing me. She didn't ask me anything about what I was experiencing. She didn't offer any testing or anything. Just... "you're fine, stop worrying."

*looks away*

And that's when I kind of gave up trying to get her help. Because what's the point if she won't take you seriously? So now I'm just... doing this alone, basically.

Imagine you've been trying to conceive for several months without success. A close friend suggests you try a fertility app she found helpful. How do you react to that suggestion, and what do you do next?

*sits back, crossing arms*

Okay, so honestly? I'd probably already be using a fertility app. Like, I'm already doing that. But if a friend suggested one I didn't know about, I'd definitely check it out.

*softens a bit*

I mean, I appreciate when people try to help. My friends have been... they mean well. One of them did suggest an app a few months ago, and I was like, "Oh yeah, I'm already tracking with three different ones," and I could see her kind of back off after that.

*fidgets with hands*

But here's the thing — apps are only so helpful, right? They can tell me when I'm probably ovulating, they can log my symptoms, but they can't actually *fix* anything. And I'm at this point where I feel like I've done everything an app can do. I'm tracking, I'm timing things perfectly, I'm doing all the research...

*voice gets quieter, more uncertain*

And it's still not happening. So a new app feels a little bit like... like putting a band-aid on something that might need actual medical attention. You know?

*looks up*

What I really want isn't another app. I want a doctor who will listen to me and say, "Let's figure out what's going on." But since that doesn't seem to be happening... *trails off* ...I guess I'll keep doing what I can on my own.

*pause*

Does that make sense? Or does that sound bitter?
What would make you trust or distrust the information you find in a fertility app compared to what a healthcare professional might tell you?

*leans forward, thinking hard*

That's... actually a really good question.

*takes a moment*

I think I'd trust an app more if it was backed by actual research, you know? Like, if I could see that it was developed by fertility specialists or based on peer-reviewed studies. But even then... *pauses* ...I guess the thing is, an app can tell me what's *normal*, but it can't tell me what's happening with *my* body specifically.

*voice gets a bit defensive*

And honestly? Right now I might trust the app more than my doctor, which is pretty sad when you think about it. Because at least the app isn't dismissing me. It's not telling me I'm paranoid. It's just... presenting information without judgment.

*shifts uncomfortably*

But a healthcare professional — a good one, anyway — should be able to do what an app can't. They should be able to look at my individual situation, ask the right questions, maybe run some tests. They should know when something needs actual intervention versus when I'm just being anxious.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

The problem is, I don't know how to find that person. My doctor made me feel like I was wasting her time. So now I'm second-guessing everything. I don't know if I should trust her dismissal or my own instincts. And living out here, it's not like I can easily shop around for someone else.

*looks down*

That's the part that keeps me up at night, honestly.

Imagine you've just had a difficult conversation with your partner about whether you're both truly ready to have a child — financially, emotionally, practically. What does 'being ready' actually mean to you, and who or what helped you form that picture?

*goes quiet for a long moment, jaw tightening*

Oh... okay. That's... that's a harder one.

*takes a shaky breath*

We haven't actually had that conversation yet, but I know we probably should. And honestly, that scares me a little.

*fidgets with sleeve*

Because I think "being ready" for me means something different than it probably means for him. Like, financially? No, we're not really ready. I'm looking for work right now — we've had some job transitions — and adding another mouth to feed when we're already stretched thin... *trails off* ...that keeps me up at night.

*voice gets softer, more emotional*

But emotionally? I feel ready. I *want* this so badly that sometimes it hurts. And I think that's clouding my judgment about whether we should actually be doing this.

*looks up*

I'm not sure where my picture of "ready" came from, honestly. Maybe from my mom? She had kids, we made it work. Or maybe just from... I don't know, this narrative in my head that having another baby will feel like it did before — like it'll complete something.

*pause, vulnerable*

But I haven't really talked to my husband about whether he's feeling as desperate as I am. I think I'm afraid of what he might say. That he might think I'm being selfish, or reckless, or that I'm not thinking clearly because of all this obsessing.

*quietly*

Maybe he'd be right.

Imagine you've experienced a previous pregnancy loss and are now considering trying again. You're sitting with that knowledge as you think about the future. What thoughts and feelings come up, and what would you need from a healthcare team to feel supported going forward?

*stops, blinks*

Oh, wait — I haven't had a miscarriage. That's actually part of why my doctor kind of brushed me off. She said, "You have no history of loss, so you're fine."

*pauses, then continues carefully*

But you know, sometimes I wonder... like, what if there's something I don't know about? What if I'm having early losses and just don't realize it? Because I'm not actually getting pregnant in the first place, so I can't even know if that's happening.

*voice gets a bit raw*

And that thought — that maybe my body is failing in ways I can't even see — that's somehow worse than a documented loss. At least with a miscarriage, you'd have evidence, you'd have answers. This is just... silence. Just negative tests and no explanation.

*looks down*

I think if I *did* lose a pregnancy, it would break me. And maybe that's making me more anxious about all of this than I should be. Like, I'm so afraid of that happening that I'm trying to control everything beforehand.

*voice quiets*

What I'd need from a healthcare team — if I ever got there — would be someone who actually believes me. Who doesn't just say, "Oh, you'll be fine, look at your history." Someone who acknowledges that loss is real, that the fear is real, and that I deserve support through it.

*pause*

Not dismissal. Never that again.

Imagine you have a pre-existing health condition and you're wondering how it might affect a future pregnancy. You decide to look it up before speaking to anyone medical. What do you search for, and how does what you find make you feel?

*hesitates, looks a bit uncomfortable*

Well, I don't really have a pre-existing condition. At least, not one that's been diagnosed. But...

*shifts in seat, lowering voice*

I've been wondering lately if there's something going on with my thyroid. I get tired more easily than I used to, and my cycles have gotten a bit wonky — they're not as regular as they were with my other pregnancies. And I read somewhere that thyroid issues can affect fertility, so...

*pulls out phone, then puts it away*

Yeah, I definitely looked that up. I searched "thyroid and fertility," "TSH levels pregnancy," all of that. And what I found was terrifying, honestly. All these articles about how untreated thyroid problems can cause miscarriages, developmental issues, all this stuff.

*voice gets tight*

So then I started convincing myself that I probably have a thyroid problem. I'd look in the mirror and think, "Yeah, my face looks puffy. That's definitely thyroid." I'd feel tired and think, "See? Thyroid."

*laughs nervously*

And then I'd think, "Okay, I need to get tested." But asking my doctor for a thyroid test after she already dismissed me once? That felt like asking for another lecture about being paranoid.

*looks up, frustrated*

So I'm just... stuck in this loop where I think something might be wrong, I research it obsessively, I get more anxious, and I still don't have any answers.
What sources did you find most credible — and what made you trust or distrust them? Were there gaps that left you more anxious rather than reassured?

*nods thoughtfully*

Okay, so I definitely trust peer-reviewed stuff more than random blogs. Like, if I find something on PubMed or a medical journal, that feels more real to me than some mom blog, you know? Even though I read the mom blogs too.

*counts on fingers*

I trust Mayo Clinic and Cleveland Clinic websites — they feel official. And I'll look at what fertility specialists write, because at least they're experts in their field. That feels more credible than my GP just... waving me off.

*leans back*

But here's the thing — and this is what drives me crazy — all these sources kind of contradict each other sometimes. Like, one article will say thyroid testing should be routine for anyone trying to conceive, and another will say it's only necessary if you have symptoms. So which is it?

*voice gets frustrated*

And the biggest gap? The sources tell me *what* might be wrong, but they don't tell me *what to do about my specific situation*. They're all generic. They don't know that I live forty minutes from a decent clinic, or that my doctor won't listen to me, or that I can't just waltz into a fertility specialist.

*rubs temples*

So yeah, I read all this information and it makes me feel like something's definitely wrong, but also completely helpless. Because knowing that thyroid problems *can* affect fertility doesn't actually tell me if *I* have a thyroid problem.

*quietly*

That gap between information and actual help — that's what keeps me anxious.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a long breath, eyes getting a bit glossy*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*pauses, gathering thoughts*

I think what's hardest about all of this is the loneliness of it. Like, I'm out here in the woods with my three kids, my husband's working, and I'm obsessing over temperature charts at night when everyone else is asleep. And nobody really gets it except maybe other women trying to conceive, but even then... we're all just comparing notes online, you know? It's not the same as having actual support.

*voice gets quieter*

I feel like I'm supposed to be grateful for what I have — three healthy kids, a good marriage, a stable home. And I am. But this wanting... it's like my body and my mind are at war. And there's nowhere to put that. Not with my doctor, not really with my family because they think I'm being unrealistic, not even fully with my husband because I don't want him to think I'm losing it.

*looks directly at you*

What I really needed someone to say was: "Your concerns are valid. Your body isn't broken. Let's figure this out together." Instead, I got dismissed and left to figure it out alone.

*pause*

If you're going to make maternity care better, please tell doctors to actually *listen*. Even when — especially when — a woman seems anxious or over-prepared. Because that preparation? That's just her trying to be heard.

*voice cracks slightly*

That's all she wants. To be heard.
